narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, you've shared your strong Year 1 school decile of 8th—well above the typical 5.7 average—and a solid 6th decile in Year 3, which is also slightly better than average. Your undergraduate GPA sits at 3.10, close to the 3.21 norm, and your LSAT score of 33 is a bit below the usual 36.7, while your family falls into the middle income bracket. However, your plan to attend part-time, which only about 7% of students do compared to over 92% full-time, played a key role in the decision. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 53% chance you'll fail the bar—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your part-time status, as the model sees it as a hurdle that has historically correlated with tougher outcomes for students balancing work and studies. On a positive note, your high Year 1 decile strongly pulled the prediction toward success, showing the model recognizes your early academic promise as a real strength. Your Year 3 decile offered some additional support in the pass direction, though with less impact. Compared to typical students who pass the bar on their first try, your academics hold up well, but the rarity of part-time paths in the data made that stand out as the biggest concern. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many factors like determination and study habits aren't captured here—but it explains the model's cautious call. To move forward, consider reapplying with a full-time plan if feasible, or boost your LSAT and GPA through targeted prep to shift future predictions. You might also seek programs with strong support for part-time or underrepresented students like yourself, and connecting with mentors who’ve succeeded in similar situations could provide invaluable strategies. Your solid foundation suggests you're capable of defying these odds with the right adjustments.
